basic_info.name: Quantum Dot Ligand Exchange Solvent
basic_info.name_ja: 量子ドットリガンド交換溶媒
basic_info.description: Colloidal quantum dots (QDs) are stabilized by surface ligands whose HSP must match the processing solvent to maintain colloidal stability. During ligand exchange — replacing long-chain native ligands (e.g., oleic acid) with short-chain or inorganic ligands for improved charge transport in devices — the solvent must simultaneously dissolve the incoming ligand, solvate the QD with its new surface chemistry, and ideally be a poor solvent for the outgoing ligand to drive the exchange. HSP matching between the QD-ligand shell and the exchange solvent governs aggregation, precipitation, and the completeness of ligand exchange, directly affecting QD film quality in LEDs, solar cells, and photodetectors. This framework enables systematic solvent selection for each step of QD processing rather than relying on empirical trial-and-error.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: QDs are effectively colloidal particles whose dispersibility depends on the HSP match between the ligand shell (the outermost organic or inorganic layer) and the solvent. The native synthesis ligands (oleic acid, oleylamine, trioctylphosphine oxide) have characteristic HSP, and solvents with low Ra to these ligands maintain stable dispersions. During ligand exchange, the QD surface transitions from one ligand type to another (e.g., oleic acid to mercaptopropionic acid, or from organic to halide/inorganic ligands). The exchange solvent must: (1) have RED < 1 relative to the incoming ligand to keep it dissolved and available for exchange, (2) have RED < 1 relative to the QD with partially exchanged ligand shell to prevent aggregation during the process, and (3) ideally have RED > 1 relative to the outgoing ligand-QD combination to thermodynamically drive precipitation/phase separation of the native ligand. Two-phase ligand exchange protocols explicitly exploit HSP differences: QDs transfer from a nonpolar phase (matching native ligands) to a polar phase (matching new ligands) at the interface. HSP distance also predicts the colloidal stability of the final QD ink for deposition.
hsp_principle.key_equation: Ra = sqrt(4*(dD_solv - dD_ligand)^2 + (dP_solv - dP_ligand)^2 + (dH_solv - dH_ligand)^2); RED_incoming = Ra_incoming/R0_incoming; RED_outgoing = Ra_outgoing/R0_outgoing
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: RED < 1.0 for incoming ligand-solvent compatibility; RED > 1.2 for outgoing ligand-solvent incompatibility to drive exchange; RED < 0.8 for final QD ink colloidal stability [uncertain]

required_inputs: HSP values (dD, dP, dH) of native (outgoing) ligand
HSP values (dD, dP, dH) of target (incoming) ligand
HSP values (dD, dP, dH) of exchange solvent(s)
R0 for QD-ligand colloidal stability sphere
QD core composition and size (affects surface energy)
data_sources: HSPiP database for common solvents and small organic molecules
Group contribution estimation for ligand molecules (oleic acid, thiols, amines, carboxylates)
Experimental dispersion stability testing (centrifugation, DLS) in solvent panels
Published QD-solvent compatibility studies in nanocrystal literature
IGC or contact angle measurements for QD surface characterization
complementary_parameters: QD core size and size distribution
Ligand grafting density on QD surface
Solvent boiling point and vapor pressure (for film deposition)
Solvent viscosity (affects processing and film quality)
Dielectric constant of solvent (affects electrostatic stabilization for charged ligands)
Ligand binding energy to QD surface (thermodynamic driving force for exchange)

limitations: QD surface is not a bulk material — effective HSP of a ligand-capped nanoparticle surface is size-dependent and curvature-dependent
Ligand exchange involves kinetic barriers (activation energy for ligand desorption/adsorption) not captured by thermodynamic HSP
Inorganic ligands (halides, metal chalcogenide complexes) are poorly described by organic HSP parameters
Mixed ligand shells during incomplete exchange have ill-defined composite HSP
Electrostatic stabilization (for charged ligand-capped QDs in polar solvents) operates outside HSP framework
Quantum confinement and surface trap states affected by ligand exchange are not predicted by HSP
known_failure_modes: Inorganic ligand (e.g., S2-, SCN-) exchange in formamide/DMSO where ionic interactions dominate over van der Waals HSP matching
Large QDs (>10 nm) where gravitational settling competes with colloidal stability regardless of HSP match
Ligand exchange at elevated temperatures where rapid kinetics cause uncontrolled aggregation despite favorable HSP
Multi-shell QDs (e.g., CdSe/CdS/ZnS) where surface composition differs from simple ligand model
validation_method: Dynamic light scattering (DLS) for colloidal stability and aggregation monitoring
UV-Vis and photoluminescence spectroscopy (QD optical properties as proxy for surface quality)
FTIR/NMR to confirm ligand exchange completeness
Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) for ligand content quantification
Thin film characterization (AFM, SEM) for QD film morphology after deposition

key_references: Shen, Y.; Gee, M.Y.; Greytak, A.B. 'Purification technologies for colloidal nanocrystals', Chem. Commun., 2017, 53, 827-841. DOI: 10.1039/C6CC07998A

�Nag, A.; Kovalenko, M.V.; Lee, J.S.; Liu, W.; Spokoyny, B.; Talapin, D.V. 'Metal-free Inorganic Ligands for Colloidal Nanocrystals: S2-, HS-, Se2-, HSe-, Te2-, OH-, NH2-', J. Am. Chem. Soc., 2011, 133, 10612-10620. DOI: 10.1021/ja2029415
Hansen, C.M.; Smith, A.L. 'Using Hansen solubility parameters to correlate solubility of C60 fullerene in organic solvents and in polymers', Carbon, 2004, 42, 1591-1597
Mori, Y.; Yamada, K. 'Hansen Solubility Parameters for Selection of Dispersing Solvents for Quantum Dots', J. Chem. Eng. Data, 2020, 65, 5434-5441 [uncertain]
Boles, M.A.; Ling, D.; Hyeon, T.; Talapin, D.V. 'The surface science of nanocrystals', Nature Mater., 2016, 15, 141-153. DOI: 10.1038/nmat4526
literature.regulatory_relevance: No established regulatory framework for HSP in QD processing. RoHS restricts cadmium-based QDs driving adoption of InP and perovskite QDs. REACH governs solvent use in QD manufacturing. OSHA/exposure limits apply to exchange solvents (formamide, DMF, hydrazine). HSP-guided solvent substitution supports compliance with green chemistry initiatives.

industry_sectors: Display technology (QD-LED/QLED TVs, QD color conversion films)
Photovoltaics (QD solar cells, luminescent solar concentrators)
commercial_tools: Materials Studio (atomistic simulation of QD-ligand-solvent systems)
